lecture_title: Impact Analysis, Software Processes & Continuous Integration

questions.stem: You are running Impact Analysis on the store graph and you have just marked Item as CHANGED. Item's only neighbours are Inventory and Price. Which classes do you schedule next?

options.text: Nothing yet; you must inspect Item again first
Inventory and Price, both marked NEXT
Store and Cashiers, both marked NEXT
Only Inventory, because Price is a leaf

questions.explanation: After a class is marked CHANGED you schedule its BLANK neighbours as NEXT; Item's neighbours are Inventory and Price. (ImpactAnalysis p.7, p.12)

questions.stem: You inspect a NEXT class and conclude it needs no new code of its own, yet change clearly passes through it to reach its other neighbours. Which mark do you assign so propagation continues?
questions.explanation: A relay/mailman class is marked PROPAGATING: it needs no code change but its BLANK neighbours must still be scheduled. UNCHANGED would wrongly stop the spread. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: Mid-analysis your NEXT queue is empty but two classes are still BLANK and were never reached from any CHANGED or PROPAGATING class. What do you do?

options.text: Mark the two BLANK classes UNCHANGED to be safe before stopping
Re-seed them as CHANGED and continue the loop
Mark them NEXT so every class is inspected before stopping
Stop; the estimated impact set is the CHANGED/PROPAGATING classes, and untouched BLANK classes stay out of it

questions.explanation: The loop ends when no NEXT remain; classes never reached stay BLANK and are simply not in the estimated set. You do not force-inspect unreached classes. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: As a solo programmer you finished one change at 4:55 pm just before your shift ends. The deck's baseline guidance says you should do what now rather than tomorrow?

options.text: Create the baseline now; postponing baselines is not recommended
Postpone the baseline to batch it with tomorrow's change
Skip the baseline because only one change was made
Wait until the release plan date to baseline

questions.explanation: Baselines are scheduled at regular intervals (e.g. end of each shift) and postponing is explicitly not recommended. (IntroSwProcesses p.26)

questions.stem: Your small team makes every decision by consensus, nobody specialises, and everyone is just a 'programmer'. Which team process are you running?
questions.explanation: AIP: small/medium team, consensus decisions, no specialisation, the single programmer role. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: You join a large project where managers direct work, programmers hold specialised roles (tester, architect), and additionally nobody may commit without an architect's or code owner's permission. Which process is this?
questions.explanation: The explicit 'permission to commit' gate via architects/code owners is CIP's signature feature on top of DIP-style direction and specialisation. (TeamProcesses p.15-16)

questions.stem: Your build passes on your laptop but fails on the team's build server. According to the deck, whose result is authoritative?

options.text: Your laptop, since you can reproduce the build there
Whichever machine ran the most recent build
Neither; you re-run until both agree
The build server, which only takes code from the repository and is the final authority

questions.explanation: The repo is the source of record and the build server settles disputes as the final authority, defeating the 'builds on my box' excuse. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: You are the programmer in the Interactive IA swimlane. The computer hands you a NEXT class. Which decision is yours to make rather than the computer's?

options.text: Whether the class is INSPECTED, PROPAGATING, or CHANGED
Maintaining the queue of NEXT classes

questions.explanation: In Interactive IA the computer manages state (BLANK init, the NEXT queue, applying marks) while the programmer supplies the judgement of each selected class. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: It is 9 am and your agile team gathers briefly to assign today's change requests, surface blockers, and flag any code needing refactoring. Which AIP meeting is this?
questions.explanation: The daily meeting does daily assignment of change requests, surfaces problems, identifies refactoring needs, and gives early warning. (TeamProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Your CI build currently runs only unit tests in under a minute. A teammate proposes adding a full end-to-end system-test suite that takes 40 minutes to every commit build. Per the deck's priorities, what is the main concern?

options.text: Unit tests must be removed before system tests can be added
It violates 'keep the build fast'; system tests are slow and unit tests are best for verifying builds
System tests cannot run on a build server at all

questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast/focused and the best for verifying builds; slow system tests on every commit fight the 'keep it fast' principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.9, p.11)

questions.stem: You're scoping a JHotDraw change in the Analysis Lab. After concept location and IA you must hand in a portfolio table. What does each row record?

options.text: The git commit hash for every changed file
The runtime of each unit test
A package name with the number of classes visited and comments

questions.explanation: The Analysis Lab portfolio table lists package name, number of classes, and comments after running the Fig 7.9 algorithm. (AnalysisLab p.1-2)

questions.stem: You manage a release and notice 'remaining effort' is steadily dropping toward zero, but 'total effort to reach the goal' keeps climbing snapshot after snapshot. What should you conclude?

options.text: Scope/estimates are inflating; the goalpost is moving even as work gets done
The team is ahead of schedule with nothing to worry about
The two rows are measuring the same thing and can be ignored

questions.explanation: Falling remaining effort is good, but a rising total-effort-to-goal means estimates/scope are inflating — the classic slipping-release signal. (IntroSwProcesses p.29, p.31)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the inspection marks used in the IA marking algorithm?
questions.explanation: The marks are BLANK, NEXT, CHANGED, UNCHANGED, PROPAGATING, INSPECTED. COMMITTED is not a mark. (ImpactAnalysis p.11-12, p.16)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five principles of Continuous Integration?

options.text: Deploy automatically to production after every commit

questions.explanation: The five principles are stability-based environments, maintain a repository, commit frequently/build every commit, self-testing build, keep the build fast. Auto-deploy to prod is not among them. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which item is NOT one of the four value statements of the Agile Manifesto?

options.text: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Responding to change over following a plan

questions.explanation: The fourth value is customer collaboration over contract negotiation — the opposite of 'detailed contracts over informal agreements'. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: A scrum-style daily AIP meeting does four things in the deck. Which proposed activity is the odd one out that belongs to CIP instead?

options.text: Granting formal permission to commit code to the mainline
Giving early warning when something goes wrong

questions.explanation: Permission-to-commit is the CIP safeguard, not a daily-meeting function. The daily meeting assigns requests, flags refactoring, and warns early. (TeamProcesses p.7, p.16)

questions.stem: Which class does the IA algorithm NOT need to inspect, given the store graph seeded at Item where Store turns out PROPAGATING and Cashiers UNCHANGED?

options.text: CashierRecord, which is never reached and stays BLANK

questions.explanation: Because Cashiers ends UNCHANGED it never schedules its neighbour CashierRecord, which stays BLANK and is never inspected. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four forms in which a process can be discussed?
questions.explanation: The four forms are model, enactment, performance, and plan. 'Compilation' is not among them. (IntroSwProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: Which mark does NOT extend the inspection frontier (i.e. does not cause any new NEXT classes to be scheduled)?
questions.explanation: Only CHANGED and PROPAGATING classes schedule their BLANK neighbours; UNCHANGED/INSPECTED classes do not expand the frontier. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT named anywhere in the CI deck's tooling slides?
questions.explanation: The deck names JUnit/NUnit/Selenium (frameworks), Findbugs/PMD/Checkstyle/Sonar (static analysis), and Cobertura/Emma/Clover (coverage); Jenkins is not listed. (ContinuousIntegration p.12-14)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a role described in the directed/centralized team processes?
questions.explanation: Named roles include developer, tester, architect, product manager, process manager, and code owner. 'Release Auditor' is invented. (TeamProcesses p.12-14, p.17)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a level in the process granularity hierarchy?
questions.explanation: The levels are lifecycle, stage, process, task, subtask/phase, step/action. 'Module' is not a granularity level. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In the John-mailman-Paul analogy, which statement is NOT true of the mailman?

options.text: The mailman carries the letter from John to Paul
The mailman interacts with both John and Paul
The change relays through the mailman without originating there
The mailman's own life changes substantially as a result of the loan

questions.explanation: The change originates with John and ends at Paul; the mailman merely relays it and is not itself substantially changed — that is the whole point of PROPAGATING. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: Which statement about LOC is NOT supported by the deck?

options.text: A precise count such as 23,418 LOC should be reported exactly for accuracy
LOC is the most commonly used size measure
LOC is very inaccurate across languages and styles
Only the one or two most significant digits are meaningful

questions.explanation: Because only 1-2 significant digits are meaningful, you report '23 KLOC', not '23,418 LOC'. (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between the initial impact set and the estimated impact set?

options.text: The estimated set is produced by Verification, the initial set by IA
The initial set is the Concept-Location seed; the estimated set is the full predicted set after IA expands outward
The initial set is larger and IA shrinks it to the estimated set
Both are identical; the two names are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Concept Location yields the initial (seed) set; IA grows it into the estimated impact set by propagation. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: How does a dependency differ from a coordination as flavours of class interaction?

options.text: A dependency never propagates change; coordination always does
They are two names for the same relation
A dependency is a contract where one class depends on another; coordination is sharing coding/schedule/dataflow
A dependency is undirected; coordination is directional

questions.explanation: Dependency = one class depends on another via a contract; coordination = they share coding, schedule, or dataflow (e.g. via a third class's body). (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.8)

questions.stem: Comparing the dependency diagram and the interaction diagram for the same classes C, A, B: which contrast is correct?

options.text: The interaction diagram uses directed arrows; the dependency diagram uses undirected lines
Both diagrams are identical because dependencies are interactions
The dependency diagram adds an A-B edge that the interaction diagram omits
The dependency diagram uses directed arrows (C->A, C->B); the interaction diagram uses undirected lines and adds an A-B edge

questions.explanation: Dependency arrows are directed (C depends on A and B); interactions are undirected and add A-B because A and B coordinate through C. (ImpactAnalysis p.9)

questions.stem: Which single line correctly contrasts AIP, DIP, and CIP?

options.text: AIP = consensus/no specialisation; DIP = manager-directed/specialised; CIP = DIP plus a permission-to-commit gate
AIP = manager-directed; DIP = consensus; CIP = open source
All three are identical except for team size
AIP = commit safeguard; DIP = no roles; CIP = solo

questions.explanation: AIP is consensus with no specialisation; DIP adds manager direction and specialised roles; CIP adds the architect/code-owner commit safeguard. (TeamProcesses p.4, p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: How do unit tests and system tests differ in the CI self-testing-build discussion?

options.text: System tests are the best for verifying builds; unit tests are too slow
Unit tests are fast/focused with no DB or file system; system tests are end-to-end and take minutes to hours
Unit tests are end-to-end and slow; system tests are fast and focused
Both touch the database and run at the same speed

questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast, focused, no I/O — best for verifying builds; system tests are end-to-end and slow. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: What distinguishes a process model from its enactment?

options.text: The model is the collected measures; the enactment is the future plan
They are synonyms in the deck
The model is the blueprint; the enactment is the actual run including inevitable deviations and exceptions
The model is the actual run; the enactment is the blueprint

questions.explanation: Model = prescription/blueprint; enactment = the process as actually run, with deviations and exceptions. (IntroSwProcesses p.5)

questions.stem: What is the difference between 'total time' and 'clean time' in a SIP time log?

options.text: Clean time includes interruptions; total time excludes them
They are equal whenever no defects are found
Total time is the average; clean time is the median
Total time includes interruptions; clean time = end - start - interruptions

questions.explanation: Total time includes all interruptions; clean time is the focused work time, end - start - interruptions. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: How does a product manager differ from a process manager in a directed process?

options.text: Product managers make strategic decisions; process managers assign tasks and control the process
Product managers assign tasks; process managers set strategy
Both verify the baseline like testers
Product managers approve commits; process managers write code

questions.explanation: Product managers handle strategic decisions; process managers assign tasks and control the process. (TeamProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: How do the two estimation techniques, analogy and decomposition, differ?

options.text: Analogy applies only to epics; decomposition only to bugs
Analogy estimates a phase from similar past phases; decomposition breaks a change into phases and sums their estimates
Analogy sums per-phase estimates; decomposition copies a past phase's time
Both require unprecedented tasks to work

questions.explanation: Analogy reuses similar past phases' times; decomposition splits the change and sums per-phase estimates (errors may compensate). (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: How does the team-processes notion of code ownership contrast with CI's stance on ownership?

options.text: CI assigns each module a single owner; team processes share everything
Team code ownership has individuals specialise in code parts (coordination risk); CI says the team owns the code, not the individual
Both insist the individual owns their files exclusively

questions.explanation: Team code ownership = individuals specialise (coordination can become a problem); CI explicitly says the team owns the code, not the individual. (TeamProcesses p.17; ContinuousIntegration p.5)

questions.stem: How does 'performance' differ from 'plan' among the four process forms?

options.text: Performance is the future expectation; plan is the past measures
Both are blueprints of the tasks
Performance is the model; plan is the enactment
Performance is the measures collected from an enacted process; plan is the expected future performance

questions.explanation: Performance = measures an observer collects from the running process; plan = expected future performance and the decisions about alternatives. (IntroSwProcesses p.6)

questions.stem: Comparing the basic IA algorithm with the propagating-classes variant, what does the variant add?

options.text: The PROPAGATING (mailman) case, which relays change onward without itself changing
A way to seed multiple initial impact sets at once
Automatic commit permission for changed classes

questions.explanation: The propagating-classes variant adds the PROPAGATING branch so relay classes still schedule their neighbours; the basic version only knows CHANGED/UNCHANGED. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: A class needs no new code itself, yet because change passes through it you must still inspect its other neighbours. Which mark names this situation?
questions.explanation: That is exactly the PROPAGATING (mailman) class — a relay that needs no code but extends the frontier. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: A measure reads roughly 0.1 defects per KLOC and is described as the cutting edge of what can be achieved. Which class of software does it describe?
questions.explanation: ~0.1 defects/KLOC is the avionics figure (cutting edge); good-quality software is around 2.0/KLOC. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: CIP and DIP can have the same managers, developers, and testers. Which structural element, present in the CIP model diagram, is the one thing that tells them apart?

options.text: A product backlog feeding an iteration backlog
A build step handled by testers
A release run by the process manager
A 'permission to commit' step gated by architects and code owners

questions.explanation: All the listed elements appear in DIP too; only the architect/code-owner permission-to-commit gate is unique to CIP. (TeamProcesses p.11, p.16)

questions.stem: You see a measure defined as 'end - start - time of interruptions'. Which quantity is being computed?
questions.explanation: Clean time = end - start - interruptions; total time includes interruptions. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: A planning artifact lists each planned change as a row and snapshots its remaining effort in columns after x hours. What is it?
questions.explanation: That describes the release backlog table, which reveals on-time/late/incomplete releases. (IntroSwProcesses p.28)

questions.stem: A set is produced by Concept Location and serves as the seed that Impact Analysis expands. Name it.
questions.explanation: Concept Location produces the initial impact set; IA grows it into the estimated impact set. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: An agile micro-cycle is described as DEVELOP -> REACT -> MODIFY. Which process uses it?
questions.explanation: DEVELOP -> REACT -> MODIFY is AIP's micro-cycle. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: A graph-quality concept finds 'hotspots' of low testing combined with high complexity. Which tool is named for it?
questions.explanation: SONAR finds hotspots of low testing plus high complexity; Cobertura measures coverage; Checkstyle checks compliance. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: A workproduct of SIP 'represents the vision for the future of the software' and includes bugs and new ideas. Which is it?
questions.explanation: The product backlog represents the vision for the software's future, including bugs and new demands. (IntroSwProcesses p.9)

questions.stem: A class is neither origin nor final target of a change but sits on the path the change travels. Which mark and analogy describe it?
questions.explanation: A relay on the change path is PROPAGATING, the mailman in the John-mailman-Paul story. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: A defect-log column links a found defect back to the phase that introduced it. Which column is it?
questions.explanation: The 'origin' column records the date/task where the defect was introduced, connecting symptom to source phase. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: A developer keeps saying 'but it builds on my box.' Which CI arrangement is designed precisely to settle this dispute?

options.text: The build server, which only takes code from the repo and is the final authority
Letting each developer self-certify their machine
Running builds only weekly to reduce conflicts

questions.explanation: CI makes the repo the source of record and the build server the final authority, defeating the 'works on my machine' excuse. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: A programmer computes a step's elapsed time as end minus start but ignores the 12 minutes of interruptions. What is the consequence?

options.text: They report total time, overstating the clean (focused) work time

questions.explanation: Subtracting interruptions is required for clean time; skipping it leaves you with total time, which overstates focused work. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: During IA you wrongly mark a true mailman class UNCHANGED. What goes wrong?

options.text: All classes get re-seeded as CHANGED
Nothing; UNCHANGED and PROPAGATING behave the same
Its BLANK neighbours are never scheduled, so propagation stops early and the estimated set is too small

questions.explanation: UNCHANGED does not extend the frontier, so a mislabelled mailman halts propagation and downstream impacted classes are missed. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: A team has no build server and integrates only once at the end of the project. According to CI reasoning, what is the likely consequence?

options.text: Faster delivery because integration is deferred
Painful, large integration problems; CI exists to integrate often and reduce them

questions.explanation: CI integrates many times a day precisely because rare big-bang integration is painful; 'if it hurts, do it more often'. (ContinuousIntegration p.2, p.7)

questions.stem: Building every commit shrinks which gap, and therefore improves quality?

options.text: The gap between defect introduction and defect removal
The gap between commit and release-plan date
The gap between LOC and function points
The gap between architect and code owner

questions.explanation: Building every commit reduces the time between when a defect is introduced and when it is removed. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: In the after-100-hours release backlog, 100 hours of work were done yet remaining effort rose from 330 to 340. What caused this and what does it mean?

options.text: Estimates grew as work revealed more (e.g. cashier sessions 35->80), so the goal moved further away
The team did no work in those 100 hours
Two changes were cut from the release

questions.explanation: Several estimates inflated (inventory 30->50, sessions 35->80, etc.), pushing total-effort-to-goal to 440 and remaining up to 340 despite 100 hours done. (IntroSwProcesses p.29)

questions.stem: A change request says 'display Celsius instead of Fahrenheit', and IA finds two valid places to make it. What consequence does this present to the programmer?

options.text: A need to abandon the interaction graph
An automatic choice with no decision required
A trade-off between required effort and clarity of the resulting code
A guaranteed reduction in the estimated impact set

questions.explanation: Two candidate locations force the effort-vs-clarity trade-off (short-term vs long-term). (ImpactAnalysis p.14-15)

questions.stem: Relying only on the author's own checking of their code leads to what outcome, per the CI deck?

options.text: Over 90% of defects are removed by one person
Defect density drops to avionics levels
Many defects slip through, since individuals are under 50% efficient at finding their own bugs

questions.explanation: Individuals are <50% efficient at finding their own bugs, so multiple methods (3+) are needed for >90% removal. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: A team treats every change as a unique, unprecedented task and never makes changes more uniform. What is the planning consequence?

options.text: Planning becomes hard because only repetitions are easy to predict
Planning becomes trivial because uniqueness removes risk

questions.explanation: Repetitions are easiest to predict; unique/unprecedented tasks are hard to plan, which is why tasking makes changes more alike. (IntroSwProcesses p.22, p.24)

questions.stem: Imagine Concept Location hands the IA marking algorithm no seed class at all. What does the algorithm therefore yield?

options.text: An empty estimated impact set, since there is no CHANGED seed to expand from
The whole graph, because everything defaults to CHANGED
An error that halts the process

questions.explanation: With no seed marked CHANGED, no neighbours become NEXT, so the estimated set is empty. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: You want to estimate a new concept-location phase and you have records of several very similar past concept-location phases. Which estimation technique is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Analogy estimates a phase's time from similar past phases — ideal when comparable history exists. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: You face one large, unfamiliar change with no comparable history. Which technique best produces an estimate?

options.text: Analogy — copy a similar past phase's time
Decomposition — split it into phases and sum the per-phase estimates

questions.explanation: With no analog, decomposition breaks the change into phases whose per-phase errors may compensate. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: To promote code as its quality improves, which environment ordering is the most appropriate CI pipeline?
questions.explanation: 'Environments based on stability' promote code to stricter environments: Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)

questions.stem: To verify each CI build most effectively, which test type is the best primary choice?

options.text: No tests; rely on static analysis only
Unit tests — fast, focused, no DB or file system
Full system tests on every commit

questions.explanation: Unit tests are the best method for verifying builds because they are fast and focused with no I/O. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: A change to temperature display will recur often as new sensors and screens are added. Which alternative location best serves long-term clarity?

options.text: Hard-code Celsius strings in the UI
Leave Fahrenheit and add a note
Convert centrally where sensor data becomes temperature, keeping all temperature logic in one place

questions.explanation: Central conversion costs more effort now but keeps temperature logic in one place — better clarity for recurring future changes. (ImpactAnalysis p.14-15)

questions.stem: Your project is large with many specialised roles and managers directing work, but you do NOT need an explicit commit-permission gate. Which process fits best?
questions.explanation: DIP is manager-directed with specialised roles that scales large, without CIP's commit safeguard. (TeamProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: Which set of tools is the most appropriate choice for measuring how much of your code the unit tests exercise?
questions.explanation: Code coverage tools are Emma, Cobertura, Clover, GCC/GCOV. The others are static-analysis or test frameworks. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: You want >90% defect removal. Per the CI deck, what is the most appropriate strategy?

options.text: Use 3 or more quality methods, especially design inspections, code inspections, and testing
Rely on the author finding their own bugs
Run a single end-to-end test suite

questions.explanation: Multiple methods (3+) reach >90% removal; the most effective are design inspections, code inspections, and testing. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: For a large 'epic' change request, what is the recommended handling?

options.text: Convert it to function points first
Divide it into smaller, more uniform changes
Schedule it as a single unbroken change

questions.explanation: Tasking divides large changes (epics) into smaller, more alike changes to keep size range narrow and predictable. (IntroSwProcesses p.24)

questions.stem: To find every class a change might touch, which graph should you walk?

options.text: The dependency graph, following arrows one way
The interaction graph, because change propagates in both directions

questions.explanation: IA uses the interaction graph because interactions propagate change bidirectionally; one-way dependency arrows would miss back-propagation. (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: A student writes 'good software has ~0.1 defects/KLOC and avionics has ~2.0.' What is the correction?

options.text: The figures are swapped: good software is ~2.0/KLOC, avionics ~0.1/KLOC
Good software is 0.1 and avionics is 0.01
The numbers are correct as written

questions.explanation: Good-quality software is ~2.0 defects/KLOC; avionics (cutting edge) is ~0.1/KLOC. The student swapped them. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: A student labels the relaying 'mailman' class UNCHANGED. Why is that a misconception?

options.text: The mailman is never inspected at all
UNCHANGED stops the frontier; a mailman must be PROPAGATING so its neighbours are still inspected
The mailman should be CHANGED, never PROPAGATING
UNCHANGED and PROPAGATING are equivalent here

questions.explanation: Marking the mailman UNCHANGED halts propagation; it must be PROPAGATING to keep scheduling its BLANK neighbours. (ImpactAnalysis p.10)

questions.stem: A student reads 'Working software over comprehensive documentation' as 'documentation is worthless.' Why is that wrong?

options.text: Y is actually valued more than X
The statement is not in the Manifesto
'X over Y' means X is valued more, while there is still value in Y

questions.explanation: The Manifesto keeps the right side too: it values items on the left more, not the right not at all. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: A teammate insists 'IA just finds the concept named in the change request.' Why is this a misconception?

options.text: IA actually edits the code, not finds concepts
IA produces the initial impact set, not the estimated one
That is Concept Location; IA expands the initial set into the estimated impact set

questions.explanation: Finding the concept is Concept Location; IA takes that initial set and grows it into the estimated impact set. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: 'AIP is manager-directed with specialised roles and a commit gate.' Which corrections are needed?

options.text: AIP is consensus-based with no specialisation and no commit gate; that description fits DIP/CIP
AIP indeed has a commit gate but no managers
AIP has specialisation but no managers
The description of AIP is accurate

questions.explanation: AIP = consensus, no specialisation, single programmer role; manager direction is DIP and the commit gate is CIP. (TeamProcesses p.4, p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: A student claims 'IA should always pick the lowest-effort change location.' Why is this mistaken?

options.text: The two criteria (effort and clarity) genuinely conflict; least effort often scatters logic and hurts long-term clarity
Lowest effort is always best for clarity too

questions.explanation: Effort and clarity conflict; the lowest-effort patch (e.g. UI-only) often harms clarity, so IA surfaces both options with costs. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: A student says 'IA follows dependency arrows in one direction.' What is wrong?

options.text: Dependencies are bidirectional, interactions are not
Nothing is wrong with that statement
IA walks the interaction graph because change propagates both ways (A<->B)
IA follows arrows but only upward

questions.explanation: Interactions are bidirectional, so IA cannot just follow one-way dependency arrows — change can ripple back up. (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: A student says clean time is simply 'end minus start.' Correct them.

options.text: Clean time adds interruptions to end - start
End - start is already clean time
Clean time also subtracts interruptions: end - start - interruptions; end - start alone is total time

questions.explanation: Clean time = end - start - interruptions; end - start alone is total time. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: A student attributes 'integrate several times a day' to Grady Booch. Why is that imprecise?

options.text: Booch coined the term CI (1991) but did NOT advocate daily integration; XP/Beck did
Booch never used the term CI
Booch advocated tens of integrations per day
Beck coined the term, not Booch

questions.explanation: Booch first proposed 'CI' in 1991 without advocating daily integration; XP (Beck) advocated integrating many times per day. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: A student labels 'acceptance testing' as a process in the granularity hierarchy. Where does it actually belong?

options.text: At the task level, alongside 'software change'
At the process level, alongside SIP

questions.explanation: 'Acceptance testing' and 'software change' are tasks; SIP/AIP/DIP are processes; class inspection is a step. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: A student reports '23,418 LOC' as a precise size. What is the misconception?

options.text: Function points are more precise and should replace it
LOC cannot be measured at all
Only the one or two most significant digits are meaningful, so report '23 KLOC'
LOC must always be reported to the exact line

questions.explanation: LOC is inaccurate; only 1-2 significant digits are meaningful (23 KLOC, not 23,418 LOC). (IntroSwProcesses p.16-17)

questions.stem: Put the first three steps of the IA marking algorithm in order.

options.text: Mark the concept class CHANGED; mark all classes BLANK; stop
Mark all classes NEXT; inspect each; mark BLANK
Mark neighbours NEXT; mark all BLANK; mark seed CHANGED
Mark all classes BLANK; mark the concept-location class CHANGED; mark its BLANK neighbours NEXT

questions.explanation: The algorithm starts: BLANK everything, seed the concept-location class as CHANGED, then schedule its BLANK neighbours as NEXT. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Within the IA loop, after selecting a NEXT class and finding it CHANGED, what is the next action before re-testing for NEXT?

options.text: Mark its BLANK neighbours as NEXT

questions.explanation: A CHANGED class spreads the change by scheduling its BLANK neighbours as NEXT, then the loop re-tests for any NEXT. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: In the SIP enactment table, which ordering of phase abbreviations is correct for one change?

options.text: Ini, Pri, IA, CL, Ref, Act, Ex, Base
Pri, Ini, CL, IA, Ref, Ex, Act, Base
CL, Pri, IA, Ini, Act, Ref, Base, Ex
IA, CL, Ini, Pri, Base, Act, Ref, Ex

questions.explanation: The enactment sequence is Pri (prioritise), Ini, CL, IA, Ref, Ex, Act, Base. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: Mapping concept-location order: which abbreviations correspond, in order, to Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, and Actualization?
questions.explanation: CL = concept location, IA = impact analysis, Ref = (pre)factoring, Act = actualization, in that order. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the IA worked-example trace, a single box passes through which sequence of shadings?
questions.explanation: The trace shows boxes moving BLANK -> NEXT (hatched) -> CHANGED (dark)/UNCHANGED (U). (ImpactAnalysis p.13)

questions.stem: In the SIP model loop, what is the correct flow after an iteration backlog is drawn from the product backlog?

options.text: Software changes -> code update -> baseline -> iteration/release -> delivery to users
Delivery -> baseline -> code update -> software changes
Baseline -> software changes -> delivery -> code update
Iteration/release -> software changes -> baseline -> code update

questions.explanation: Sol performs software changes -> code update -> baseline -> iteration/release -> delivery, then the loop repeats. (IntroSwProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: Order the CI build cycle shown in the deck's diagram.

options.text: Commit -> Build -> Test -> Report -> back to development

questions.explanation: The CI cycle around source control is Commit -> Build -> Test -> Report -> back to development. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: When does the IA loop terminate, and why is termination guaranteed?

options.text: After exactly five iterations regardless of graph size
When the first UNCHANGED class is found
When no NEXT classes remain; each class moves BLANK->NEXT->final once in a finite graph
When all classes are CHANGED; the graph keeps growing

questions.explanation: The loop stops when no NEXT remain; termination is guaranteed because each finite-graph class transitions once. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Order the four process forms from blueprint to expected future.
questions.explanation: Model (blueprint) -> enactment (actual run) -> performance (measures) -> plan (expected future). (IntroSwProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: In the AIP iteration meeting, what is produced as its output?

options.text: An iteration backlog extracted from the product backlog

questions.explanation: The iteration meeting assesses product state and plans the next iteration, producing an iteration backlog from the product backlog. (TeamProcesses p.6)

questions.stem: Store graph edges: Store-Inventory, Store-Cashiers, Inventory-Item, Item-Price, Cashiers-CashierRecord. Seeded at Item, Inventory becomes CHANGED, Price UNCHANGED, Store PROPAGATING, Cashiers UNCHANGED. What is the estimated impact set?
questions.explanation: The estimated set is the CHANGED plus PROPAGATING classes: Item (seed), Inventory (CHANGED), Store (PROPAGATING). (ImpactAnalysis p.7, p.12)

questions.stem: On that store graph, Cashiers turned out UNCHANGED, yet the algorithm still spent effort inspecting it. Trace back: what put Cashiers on the NEXT queue in the first place?

options.text: Because every class is always inspected
Because Store was PROPAGATING and scheduled its BLANK neighbour Cashiers as NEXT
Because Cashiers was the seed class

questions.explanation: Store being PROPAGATING (the mailman) scheduled its neighbour Cashiers as NEXT — that is the value of the propagating refinement. (ImpactAnalysis p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: In the coordination code example, class C holds 'A a' and 'B b' and runs 'b.paint(a.get());' inside foo(). What does this create between a and b?

options.text: A direct dependency arrow from a to b
A dataflow, so a and b coordinate even without a direct interface dependency

questions.explanation: The line b.paint(a.get()) creates a dataflow between a and b inside C's body — coordination, not a direct dependency. (ImpactAnalysis p.8)

questions.stem: In the SB5-MAI labs, against which target codebase do students run the IA marking algorithm?
questions.explanation: The Analysis Lab runs the Fig 7.9 IA algorithm against the JHotDraw Java codebase; the store names on slides are pedagogical stand-ins. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the incomplete-release scenario, the line is drawn so items 11 (credit payment) and 12 (check payment) fall below the cut at 405 hours. What is the outcome?

options.text: The release ships without those two changes, leaving 70 of effort un-shipped
The release waits until all 12 changes are done

questions.explanation: In the incomplete release the cut leaves credit and check payment unshipped (70 of effort), and the release goes out at 405 hours without them. (IntroSwProcesses p.31)

questions.stem: You are reading a SIP time log and one row's comment reads 'Estimated set has 4 classes'. Which phase abbreviation should that row carry?
questions.explanation: IA produces the estimated impact set; the enactment table records 'Estimated set has 4 classes' at the IA step. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: A cash-register time log shows an IA step spanning 8:52 to 9:23 with 12 minutes of interruptions. Subtract the interruptions: what clean time remains?
questions.explanation: Clean time = (9:23 - 8:52) - 12 = 31 - 12 = 19 minutes. (IntroSwProcesses p.13-14)

questions.stem: Though the non-terminating loop in Cashier.get() surfaces during CL in the defect log, the log credits its origin to a different task. Which task gets the blame?
questions.explanation: The defect log records the loop's origin as Act, even though it was found during CL — linking symptom to introducing phase. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: A SIP enactment row reads 'Ex - Install new version of Bugzilla', breaking the smooth phase sequence. Which process concept does such an entry exemplify?

options.text: An exception/interruption deviating from the process model
A normal scheduled phase of every change

questions.explanation: 'Ex' is an exception/interruption — an enactment deviation from the model (here, installing new Bugzilla). (IntroSwProcesses p.11, p.5)

questions.stem: In the CI Lab, what infrastructure do students stand up for their (JHotDraw-based) project?

options.text: GitHub Actions workflow (.yml) plus a Maven build with tests

questions.explanation: The CI Lab has students add a GitHub Actions .yml and Maven build/tests, plus .maven-settings.xml for shared jars. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Given G = (X, I) with X = {A,B,C,D} and I = {(A,B),(B,C),(C,D)} (a path), what is the neighbourhood N(B)?
questions.explanation: N(B) = { Y | (B,Y) in I } = {A, C}; B touches A and C but not D. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: Trace the path graph A-B-C-D: only A is CHANGED, B comes out PROPAGATING, C comes out UNCHANGED. After marking, which classes form the estimated impact set?
questions.explanation: Estimated set = CHANGED + PROPAGATING = {A, B}; C is UNCHANGED so D is never reached. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: For the interaction graph G=(X,I), what do X and I denote?

options.text: X is inputs; I is outputs
X is the set of classes; I is the set of (undirected) interactions
X is the set of interactions; I is the set of classes
X is the initial impact set; I is the estimated set

questions.explanation: G = (X, I): X is the set of classes (vertices), I the set of interaction edges. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: A release backlog row reads: remaining effort 330, 340, 180, 70, 0 across snapshots at 0/100/285/405/475 hours, with all 12 changes shipped (planned 330). How is this release classified?

options.text: Late — effort reaches 0 only at 475 hours versus the planned 330
On time — it shipped all 12 changes

questions.explanation: All changes ship but at 475 hours versus a planned 330, so it is a late release. (IntroSwProcesses p.30)

questions.stem: A coverage view shows green and red lines with the note '1 of 2 branches missed' over addAll(int index, Collection c). What do these indicate?

options.text: Green = passing tests, red = failing tests, and one assertion failed
Green = fast lines, red = slow lines
Green = added lines, red = deleted lines in a diff
Green = covered lines, red = uncovered lines, and one of two branches was not exercised by tests

questions.explanation: In coverage tooling green = covered, red = uncovered; '1 of 2 branches missed' means a branch went untested. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: In a dependency diagram C has dashed directed arrows to A and B. What does the corresponding interaction diagram add or change?

options.text: Undirected lines C-A, C-B, plus a direct A-B edge because A and B coordinate through C
Directed arrows from A and B back to C only
It removes the C-A and C-B relations entirely
Nothing changes; the diagrams are identical

questions.explanation: The interaction diagram makes the edges undirected and adds A-B, since A and B coordinate through C's body. (ImpactAnalysis p.9)

questions.stem: A time-log row records Start, End, Interruptions (count and minutes), Step, and Comments. Which computed quantity does this row most directly support?
questions.explanation: Start/End/Interruptions feed clean time = end - start - interruptions for that step. (IntroSwProcesses p.13-14)

questions.stem: Reading a release backlog table, which two bottom rows matter most and what do their trends mean?

options.text: Defect density and clean time; both should fall
Start time and end time; their gap is clean time
Remaining effort (falling = converging) and total effort to reach goal (rising = scope inflating)
LOC and KLOC; both should rise together

questions.explanation: Watch remaining effort (toward 0 = converging) and total-effort-to-goal (rising = inflating scope/slipping release). (IntroSwProcesses p.29, p.31)

questions.stem: Given X={Store,Inventory,Item,Price,Cashiers,CashierRecord} and edges Store-Inventory, Store-Cashiers, Inventory-Item, Item-Price, Cashiers-CashierRecord, what is N(Store)?
questions.explanation: Store's edges go to Inventory and Cashiers only, so N(Store) = {Inventory, Cashiers}. (ImpactAnalysis p.7)

questions.stem: A defect-log entry shows Found(date/task), Location, Description, Origin(date/task), and Fixed date. What analytical link does the Origin field enable?

options.text: Connecting a symptom back to the phase that introduced the defect

questions.explanation: Origin ties the defect's symptom back to the introducing phase/task, supporting process improvement. (IntroSwProcesses p.20)

questions.stem: Your one-person side project has no team, yet the deck still recommends you follow a defined process. Why?

options.text: Because LOC cannot be measured otherwise
So you meet obligations: keep promises, plan the future, and manage your own resources
Because a single programmer cannot write code without one
Because solo work needs a commit-permission gate

questions.explanation: SIP exists so even a solo programmer meets obligations and manages resources rather than just reacting ad hoc. (IntroSwProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: You want to commit small, functional changes with unit tests and remember that the team, not you, owns the code. Which CI principle are you practising?

options.text: Commit frequently and build every commit

questions.explanation: Committing small functional changes with unit tests, team-owned code, is the 'commit frequently / build every commit' principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.5)

questions.stem: You are deciding the build-server hardware. Which activity does the deck warn needs 'lots and lots' of CPU?
questions.explanation: Static analysis needs lots and lots of CPU; compile+unit-test needs lots of CPU; Maven+Java needs memory. (ContinuousIntegration p.11)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT listed by the deck as a static-analysis tool?
questions.explanation: Selenium is a test framework; Findbugs, PMD, Checkstyle (and Sonar, Fortify, etc.) are static-analysis tools. (ContinuousIntegration p.12-13)

questions.stem: Which is NOT an input or output of Impact Analysis?

options.text: Class dependencies analysed to add impacted classes

questions.explanation: IA consumes the initial set and produces the estimated set by analysing dependencies; it does not take or produce a compiled binary. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: How does open-source development relate to code ownership and safeguarding per the deck?

options.text: It uses a single owner and a tiny expert team
It is safeguarded, uses code ownership, and has a wide community of variable skill
It is unsafeguarded with no code ownership

questions.explanation: Open source is characterised as safeguarded, using code ownership, with a wide community of variable skills. (TeamProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: An agile slogan justifies doing painful activities more frequently until they become routine. Which slogan is it?

options.text: 'If it hurts, do it more often'

questions.explanation: 'If it hurts, do it more often' justifies build-every-commit; difficult activities get easier with frequency. (ContinuousIntegration p.7)

questions.stem: A proverb in the planning slides stresses the repetitive nature of the process. Which is it?

options.text: 'If it hurts, do it more often'

questions.explanation: 'Repetition is the mother of skill' stresses that repetitions are the easiest things to plan. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: A team commits rarely and integrates in one big batch near release. Per CI, what does committing frequently instead achieve?

options.text: It lowers defect density to 0.1/KLOC automatically
It reduces integration problems and lets cohesive software develop more rapidly
It removes the need for a build

questions.explanation: Integrating many times a day, verified by automated builds, significantly reduces integration problems and speeds cohesive development. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: Which is the most appropriate description of why the architect can block a commit in a directed process?

options.text: To guarantee developers preserve software architecture constraints
To assign the day's change requests

questions.explanation: The architect preserves architecture constraints and approves or disapproves commits. (TeamProcesses p.13)

questions.stem: A student says 'function points are the most accurate size measure, more so than LOC.' Correct this.

options.text: Function points are exact and replace LOC
Function points equal KLOC by definition
Function points are the least accurate of all and never used
Function points correlate with LOC but are harder to compute; counts of methods/classes/files are even less accurate

questions.explanation: Function points correlate with LOC but are harder to compute; method/class/file counts are even less accurate. (IntroSwProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: In the AIP micro-cycle, which step comes first?
questions.explanation: AIP's micro-cycle is DEVELOP -> REACT -> MODIFY, so DEVELOP is first. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: In the tasking example, 'Customers can download and use sales coupons online' is decomposed. Which idea does this illustrate?

options.text: Dividing a large epic into smaller, more uniform changes

questions.explanation: The coupon example decomposes an epic (web site, manage coupons, cash at payment, usage DB) into smaller changes. (IntroSwProcesses p.24-25)

questions.stem: In the original release backlog with 12 changes, the bottom rows read remaining effort 330 and total effort to reach goal 330. What does the equality indicate at this snapshot?

options.text: No work has yet been done, so remaining equals total effort to the goal

questions.explanation: At the 0-hour snapshot, remaining effort equals the full total effort to reach the goal (both 330). (IntroSwProcesses p.28)

questions.stem: Which statement best captures what 'estimated' means in 'estimated impact set'?

options.text: It is the exact final set after all edits are verified
It is the set of classes that compiled successfully
It is identical to the initial impact set
It is a prediction made before editing, so it can be too large or too small

questions.explanation: The estimated set is a prediction; Verification later catches over- or under-estimates. (ImpactAnalysis p.3-4)

questions.stem: Two classes 'have something in common' so that change can flow either way between them. What is this relation called?
questions.explanation: Two classes interact when they have something in common; interactions propagate change in both directions. (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: What does the slogan 'Good process -> Good product' motivate?

options.text: Studying software processes as the core of software engineering

questions.explanation: 'Good process -> Good product' motivates studying processes — the core of software engineering. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: Two projects use the same process model yet need different enactments — one is a 3-person startup on a tiny app, the other a 50-person team on avionics. The deck attributes such variation to which two axes?
questions.explanation: Processes vary by team (organisation, skills) and system (technology, domain, size, quality). (IntroSwProcesses p.2)

questions.stem: What does the Agile Manifesto value over 'following a plan'?
questions.explanation: The fourth value is responding to change over following a plan. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: In what year was the Agile Manifesto created, and by how many original authors?
questions.explanation: The Agile Manifesto was developed in 2001 by 17 original authors. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: A manager asks how frequently a CI team should merge to the mainline, and what guards each merge. Which answer matches the deck?

options.text: Multiple times per day, each integration verified by an automated build

questions.explanation: CI integrates multiple times per day, each integration verified by an automated build. (ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: Your CI build has crept up to 25 minutes and developers stop running it. Which of the five CI principles is most directly being violated?
questions.explanation: 'Keep the build fast' is the fifth CI principle. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: What does the neighbourhood N(A) contain?

options.text: Every class A depends on, one way
Every class directly interacting with A

questions.explanation: N(A) = { B | (A,B) in I } — all classes directly interacting with A. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: A single programmer named Sol repeats software changes one step at a time. Which process abbreviation names this, and what does it expand to?
questions.explanation: SIP = Solo Iterative Process, one programmer repeating changes one step at a time. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Which best describes why SIP is taught before team processes?

options.text: It has the most specialised roles
It demonstrates characteristics shared by all iterative processes
It is the only process used in industry

questions.explanation: SIP demonstrates characteristics shared by all iterative processes, so it is taught first. (IntroSwProcesses p.7)

questions.stem: Which workproducts does the deck list for SIP?

options.text: Iteration meeting, daily meeting, release meeting
Findbugs report, coverage report, build log
Product backlog, software code, software documentation
Interaction graph, dependency graph, defect density

questions.explanation: SIP workproducts are the product backlog, the software code, and software documentation. (IntroSwProcesses p.9)

questions.stem: What does 'environments based on stability' describe creating?

options.text: Per-developer laptops as the source of truth
Server environments modelling code stability, promoting code to stricter ones as quality improves

questions.explanation: It models code stability across environments and promotes code to stricter ones as quality improves. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)

questions.stem: The CI lab grounds its history in three citations covering the CI definition, Booch, and XP. Which trio of reference tags are they?
questions.explanation: CILab cites [Tho] (CI definition), [Boo] (Booch 1991), and [Bec99] (XP/Beck). (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: An approach popularised CI and pushed integration to many times a day rather than the old once-a-day rhythm. Which approach was it?
questions.explanation: XP adopted CI and advocated integrating more than once per day, perhaps tens of times. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Within a directed process, which responsibility belongs to the testers?
questions.explanation: Testers verify the new baseline; approving commits is the architect, strategy is the product manager. (TeamProcesses p.12)

questions.stem: Two large teams run nearly identical directed processes, but team B additionally forbids any merge without architect/code-owner sign-off. Team B is running CIP rather than DIP because CIP adds what?

options.text: A permission-to-commit gate controlled by architects and code owners

questions.explanation: CIP adds the explicit permission-to-commit safeguard via architects/code owners on top of DIP. (TeamProcesses p.16)

questions.stem: Looking at the DIP model diagram, the Build box sits with one specialised role rather than the developers. Which role owns it?
questions.explanation: In the DIP model testers handle the build; developers run parallel software changes. (TeamProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: What additional decision beyond finding impacted classes does Impact Analysis support?

options.text: Weighing alternative locations to make the change

questions.explanation: IA also weighs alternative places to make the change, using effort vs clarity. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: When IA surfaces two places to make a change, the programmer weighs exactly two competing factors against each other. Which pair is it?

options.text: Required effort and clarity of the resulting code

questions.explanation: The two criteria are required effort of the change and clarity of the resulting code. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: The tension between the two IA criteria pits which pair of competing goals against each other?
questions.explanation: Effort (short-term) versus clarity (long-term) is the short-term/long-term conflict. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: A commit that passes the test environment is then moved to staging, and only later to production. Which CI idea does this promotion sequence put into practice?

options.text: Moving code along Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod as it proves stable
Deleting code that fails a build

questions.explanation: Stability-based environments promote code through Dev -> Test -> Stage -> Prod as quality rises. (ContinuousIntegration p.4)

questions.stem: Which statement about the four process forms is FALSE?

options.text: Enactment includes inevitable deviations and exceptions
The plan is the actual run of the process including deviations
The model is the blueprint of the tasks
Performance is the measures collected by an observer

questions.explanation: The plan is the expected future performance, not the actual run; the actual run with deviations is the enactment. (IntroSwProcesses p.5-6)

questions.stem: A revision sheet lists four claims about the team processes. Three are accurate; spot the one that wrongly swaps AIP's and DIP's defining traits.

options.text: DIP scales to large teams and systems
DIP makes decisions by consensus with no specialised roles
AIP is consensus-based with no specialisation

questions.explanation: Consensus with no specialisation describes AIP, not DIP; DIP is manager-directed with specialised roles. (TeamProcesses p.4, p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: Reading the CI deck's defect-removal figures together, what makes a self-testing build with several quality methods the better bet than relying on the author's own checks?

options.text: One method already removes >90% of defects
Authors find 90% of their own bugs
Multiple methods remove fewer defects than one
Individuals are <50% efficient at finding their own bugs, but 3+ methods reach >90% removal

questions.explanation: Individuals are <50% efficient at finding their own bugs; using 3+ methods reaches >90% defect removal. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: A tool vendor offers a fast automatic dependency-graph extractor and suggests running IA on it directly. What is the fundamental flaw in scoping IA from dependency arrows alone?

options.text: Change propagates in both directions, so one-way dependency arrows miss back-propagation
Dependency graphs cannot be drawn for Java

questions.explanation: Interactions are bidirectional; following dependency arrows one way would miss change rippling back up. (ImpactAnalysis p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: Using only the granularity hierarchy, classify these three at once: a software change, its actualization step, and the inspection of a single class. Which mapping is internally consistent and locates IA correctly?

options.text: It ranks CI tools by speed
It places software change as a task, concept location/actualization as phases, and class inspection (what IA does) as a step
It defines defect density for every phase
It lists the Agile Manifesto values

questions.explanation: The table classifies a software change as a task, its phases as subtasks, and class inspection as a step — locating IA precisely. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: A team that integrates many times daily, builds and self-tests every commit, keeps the build fast, and treats the repo plus build server as the source of truth is practising which single discipline?
questions.explanation: All of those together describe Continuous Integration. (ContinuousIntegration p.2-11)

questions.stem: On a star graph where a central class M interacts with leaves L1..L4, M is the CHANGED seed and L1 is PROPAGATING with no further neighbours, while L2,L3,L4 are UNCHANGED. What is the estimated impact set?
questions.explanation: Estimated set = CHANGED + PROPAGATING = {M, L1}; the UNCHANGED leaves are excluded and L1 has no further neighbours. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Which combination of facts correctly distinguishes total time, clean time, and the football analogy?

options.text: Total time includes interruptions; clean time excludes them; 15 minutes is a quarter's clean time though elapsed time is longer
Clean time includes interruptions; total time is the 15-minute quarter
Total time excludes interruptions; clean time includes them
Both equal the football quarter's 15 minutes

questions.explanation: Total time includes interruptions, clean time = end - start - interruptions, and a football quarter's 15 minutes is the clean time despite longer elapsed time. (IntroSwProcesses p.14)

questions.stem: Which presentation of the IA algorithm splits the work into Computer and Programmer swimlanes?
questions.explanation: Interactive IA (p.16) uses Computer vs Programmer swimlanes; the propagating-classes variant (p.12) is the abstract version. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: In Interactive IA, which mark replaces UNCHANGED at the loop-back point?
questions.explanation: The interactive variant uses INSPECTED in place of UNCHANGED at the loop-back. (ImpactAnalysis p.16)

questions.stem: What is the main risk the deck attaches to code ownership where programmers specialise in code parts?
questions.explanation: Code ownership's downside is that coordination can become a problem. (TeamProcesses p.17)

questions.stem: Which best states why process data should be collected in SIP?

options.text: Data indicate how the process is working and are the foundation for future planning
Data are required to grant commit permission
Data replace the need for a baseline

questions.explanation: Data show how the process works and underpin future planning. (IntroSwProcesses p.12)

questions.stem: A team keeps no logs of past iterations but still tries to commit to firm future dates. Per the planning slides, why is this self-defeating?

options.text: They are closely related; the future cannot be predicted with certainty without knowing the past

questions.explanation: Recording the past and planning the future are closely related; past data are good predictors. (IntroSwProcesses p.21)

questions.stem: What does the release plan primarily involve, per the deck?

options.text: Business considerations: releasing certain functionality on a certain date, with planning ensuring the promise is realistic

questions.explanation: The release plan involves business considerations and ensures the promised date/functionality is realistic. (IntroSwProcesses p.27)

questions.stem: You split a change into six phases and estimate each; some you overshoot, some you undershoot. Which property of decomposition does this illustrate as an advantage?

options.text: It needs no breakdown of the change
Per-phase estimation errors may compensate each other

questions.explanation: Decomposition sums per-phase estimates whose errors may compensate each other. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: A backlog has wildly different change sizes, from 2-hour tweaks to 200-hour epics, making estimates unreliable. Which planning activity addresses this and how?

options.text: Make changes more alike and keep a narrow size range so they are more predictable
Maximise the size variance of changes

questions.explanation: Tasking makes changes more alike, narrowing size range and improving predictability; epics are split. (IntroSwProcesses p.24)

questions.stem: Comparing the original release backlog with the after-100-hours snapshot, several estimates inflated. Which row jumped by the largest absolute amount?
questions.explanation: Cashier sessions grew 35 -> 80 (+45), the largest jump among the inflated estimates. (IntroSwProcesses p.29)

questions.stem: Reading a release backlog, which signal tells you the release will merely be late rather than ship incomplete?

options.text: Total effort to goal stays flat
Remaining effort still reaches 0 (all changes ship), but only after more hours than planned
Some items are left below the cut line and never ship

questions.explanation: Late = effort reaches 0 (all ship) but past the planned hours; incomplete = items left below the cut unshipped. (IntroSwProcesses p.30-31)

questions.stem: To keep the self-testing build fast, the deck says one test category should dominate it because that category avoids I/O entirely. Which category is it?
questions.explanation: Unit tests avoid the database and file system, so they keep the self-testing build fast and pinpoint problems. (ContinuousIntegration p.9, p.11)

questions.stem: Which two tools does the deck name as static analysis that looks for common Java bugs?
questions.explanation: Findbugs and PMD look for common Java bugs; Checkstyle checks compliance; Cobertura/Emma are coverage. (ContinuousIntegration p.10)

questions.stem: While doing the CI lab your Maven build cannot find a dependency hosted on GitHub Packages. Which project-root file fixes this by configuring access to those shared jars?
questions.explanation: The .maven-settings.xml in the project root lets the build consume shared jars from GitHub Packages; the .yml drives Actions and pom.xml builds the project. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: You want your GitHub Actions workflow to enforce 'build every commit'. Per the CILab, which trigger event should the .yml use so each proposed change is built?
questions.explanation: The CILab workflow is configured to build on every pull request with Maven, realising 'build every commit'. (CILab p.1)

questions.stem: Your finished CILab pipeline builds on every PR and runs the test suite automatically. Which two of the five CI principles has it most directly put into practice?

options.text: Keep it fast and maintain a repository
Environments based on stability and team ownership
Commit frequently and keep it fast
Build every commit and make the build self-testing

questions.explanation: Building on every PR and running tests automatically realizes 'build every commit' and 'self-testing build'. (CILab p.1; ContinuousIntegration p.3,5,9)

questions.stem: The SIP enactment step 'Ref - Extract class Session' demonstrates that refactoring of this kind occurs at what point relative to actualization?
questions.explanation: 'Ref - Extract class Session' precedes 'Act - Replace class Session', i.e. prefactoring before actualization. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: What is the comment recorded at the 'Base' (baseline) step of the SIP enactment table?

options.text: 2 regression faults added to the backlog

questions.explanation: The baseline step records '2 regression faults added to the backlog', feeding the next iteration. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: Which two marks are the only ones that, once assigned, cause a class's BLANK neighbours to be scheduled as NEXT?
questions.explanation: Only CHANGED and PROPAGATING extend the frontier; UNCHANGED/INSPECTED do not schedule neighbours. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: One kind of class interaction is directional: a contract makes one class rely on another. Which interaction is being described?
questions.explanation: A dependency is the directional flavour (one depends on the other via a contract). (ImpactAnalysis p.5)

questions.stem: A manager-directed process with specialised roles that scales to large teams is abbreviated DIP. Which expansion is correct?
questions.explanation: DIP = Directed Iterative Process. (TeamProcesses p.10)

questions.stem: A consensus-driven, role-flat process with the DEVELOP->REACT->MODIFY cycle is abbreviated AIP. Which expansion is correct?
questions.explanation: AIP = Agile Iterative Process. (TeamProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: Two developers disagree about whether the code is stable; one cites their laptop. In CI, which entity is designated to settle the dispute as the final authority?
questions.explanation: The build server is the final authority; it only gets code from the repo. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: A code-quality report cites roughly 2 defects per thousand lines. Per the deck, this density is characteristic of which quality tier?
questions.explanation: Good-quality software is about 2.0 defects/KLOC; avionics is ~0.1. (IntroSwProcesses p.19)

questions.stem: What is the deck's advice about delaying baselines until late in a release?

options.text: It is not recommended; baselines are scheduled at regular intervals
Postpone until the release date to batch them
Baselines should be created weekly only
Baselines are optional and usually skipped

questions.explanation: Baselines run at regular intervals (e.g. end of shift) and postponing is not recommended. (IntroSwProcesses p.26)

questions.stem: You have just finished Concept Location and want to know which phase to start next. Which phase immediately follows it?
questions.explanation: After Concept Location comes Impact Analysis (step 3), before Prefactoring/Actualization. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: The deck's worked example of weighing alternative change locations centres on a unit-conversion request. Which conversion drives it?
questions.explanation: The example asks to display Celsius instead of Fahrenheit, exposing two candidate change sites. (ImpactAnalysis p.14)

questions.stem: You are told three items 'SIP, AIP, DIP' all sit at the same granularity level. Which level is that?
questions.explanation: SIP, AIP, DIP are the deck's examples at the 'process' level. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: Which pair of examples sits at the COARSEST granularity level in the hierarchy?
questions.explanation: Staged and waterfall are lifecycle-level examples, the coarsest tier. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: 'Evolution' and 'servicing' are given as examples at which single granularity level?
questions.explanation: Evolution and servicing are stage-level examples in the granularity table. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: An exam answer groups 'software change' together with SIP at the process level. At which level does 'software change' actually belong?
questions.explanation: 'Software change' is a task (with acceptance testing); SIP is a process — a level coarser. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: Where on the granularity hierarchy does 'inspection of a class' belong?
questions.explanation: 'inspection of a class' is a step/action — exactly what IA does. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: Beyond producing the estimated impact set, the deck says the IA phase 'determines' two high-level things about a change. Which?

options.text: Its runtime and its memory use
Its commit permission and its owner
Its LOC and its defect density

questions.explanation: IA determines the strategy and impact of the change. (ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: A build compiles cleanly but never runs any tests, so regressions slip through. Adopting which CI principle would force the build to verify itself?
questions.explanation: 'Make the build self-testing' requires each build to run its own tests, catching regressions. (ContinuousIntegration p.3, p.9)

questions.stem: Reading the deck's numbered list of five CI principles top to bottom, which appears at position one?
questions.explanation: The list opens with 'Environments based on stability'. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: A teammate puts Findbugs in the 'coverage' column of a tooling table. Which trio would actually fill the coverage column per the deck?
questions.explanation: Coverage tools are Emma, Cobertura, Clover (and GCC/GCOV); Findbugs/PMD/Sonar are static analysis. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: Sorting the deck's tools into buckets, which trio belongs entirely in the 'test framework' bucket?
questions.explanation: JUnit, NUnit, MSTest, Selenium, FitNesse are the test frameworks; the others are static-analysis or coverage. (ContinuousIntegration p.12)

questions.stem: You are sizing a build server. The deck pairs one activity with a heavy MEMORY demand (as opposed to CPU). Which activity is it?
questions.explanation: Maven + Java is the memory-heavy activity; static analysis is the CPU-heavy one. (ContinuousIntegration p.11)

questions.stem: What does 'add testing to the build' rely on, given individual bug-finding limits?

options.text: Multiple quality methods, since 3+ methods exceed 90% defect removal
A single tester who finds all bugs

questions.explanation: Because individuals find <50% of their own bugs, 3+ methods are used for >90% removal. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: A teammate proposes seeding the IA algorithm by marking ALL classes CHANGED at the start. Why is this wrong?

options.text: All classes should be seeded PROPAGATING instead
Seeding all CHANGED is exactly correct
The seed should be marked UNCHANGED
Only the concept-location class is seeded CHANGED; seeding everything defeats the purpose of estimating a subset

questions.explanation: The algorithm seeds only the concept-location class as CHANGED, then expands; marking everything CHANGED makes the estimate the whole graph. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: If a class is selected from NEXT and inspected as UNCHANGED, what happens to its neighbours?

options.text: Nothing; UNCHANGED does not schedule any new NEXT neighbours
All its neighbours are scheduled NEXT

questions.explanation: UNCHANGED does not extend the frontier, so no neighbours are scheduled. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: A reviewer claims 'the Agile Manifesto says processes and tools are worthless.' Combining the gotcha and the value statement, what is the precise correction?

options.text: Processes and tools are not mentioned
It values individuals and interactions OVER processes and tools, while still seeing value in the right-hand items
It bans all processes and tools
It values processes and tools over individuals

questions.explanation: 'Individuals and interactions over processes and tools' means the left is valued more, not the right as worthless. (TeamProcesses p.8)

questions.stem: On the granularity ladder, which ordering is correct from coarsest to finest?

options.text: process, lifecycle, stage, task, step/action, subtask/phase
task, subtask/phase, step/action, process, stage, lifecycle
lifecycle, stage, process, task, subtask/phase, step/action
step/action, task, process, stage, subtask/phase, lifecycle

questions.explanation: Coarse to fine: lifecycle -> stage -> process -> task -> subtask/phase -> step/action. (IntroSwProcesses p.4)

questions.stem: A late release ships all 12 changes at 475 hours (planned 330) while an incomplete release ships at 405 hours minus items 11-12. Which single difference distinguishes them?

options.text: Whether the build server is used
Whether AIP or DIP is used
Whether remaining effort reaches 0 (late) or items are left below the cut unshipped (incomplete)

questions.explanation: Late = all ship but past plan (effort hits 0 late); incomplete = some items cut and never ship. (IntroSwProcesses p.30-31)

questions.stem: Two locations can implement a temperature change; choosing the lower-effort UI patch repeatedly over time produces what long-term effect, per the criteria?

options.text: Scattered temperature logic across display sites, hurting clarity and raising future change cost
A smaller estimated impact set permanently

questions.explanation: Repeated least-effort UI patches scatter temperature logic, the clarity cost of favouring short-term effort. (ImpactAnalysis p.15)

questions.stem: In the after-100-hours backlog the bottom rows read remaining 340 and total-to-goal 440. What story do these two numbers together tell?

options.text: Two items were cut from scope
100 hours were done (440-340) but remaining rose above the original 330 because estimates inflated
The release is complete with 0 remaining
No work was done in 100 hours

questions.explanation: Total-to-goal 440 minus remaining 340 = 100 hours done, yet remaining exceeds the original 330 — scope inflated. (IntroSwProcesses p.29)

questions.stem: According to the deck, examining projects that FAILED chiefly teaches you what?

options.text: The exact defect density of success
The problems that led to failure

questions.explanation: Unsuccessful projects reveal the problems that led to failure; successful ones give prescriptions. (IntroSwProcesses p.1)

questions.stem: SIP works for one programmer, yet the course then introduces team processes. What single driver does the deck give for moving beyond a solo programmer?

options.text: Solo programmers cannot use version control
Teams remove the need for a product backlog
Projects often need more effort than one programmer can supply

questions.explanation: Most projects need more effort than a solo programmer can handle, so programmers form teams. (TeamProcesses p.2)

questions.stem: A DIP team adds dedicated testers and an architect on top of plain developers. According to the deck, what is the expected effect of this specialization?

options.text: It eliminates the need for a process manager

questions.explanation: The deck states specialization increases effectiveness, justifying additional specialised roles. (TeamProcesses p.12)

questions.stem: Which best explains why the neighbourhood is the unit the IA algorithm expands by?

options.text: Neighbours are inspected only at the end
When a class is found changed, its neighbours become the next candidates to inspect
The neighbourhood equals the whole graph

questions.explanation: A changed class's neighbours are the next inspection candidates, so N(A) is the expansion unit. (ImpactAnalysis p.6)

questions.stem: In the marking algorithm, a class that has been queued for the programmer to inspect but not yet judged carries which mark?
questions.explanation: NEXT is the 'scheduled for inspection' mark, sitting between BLANK and a final verdict. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Which mark means a class was never inspected and is not scheduled?
questions.explanation: BLANK means never inspected and not scheduled. (ImpactAnalysis p.11)

questions.stem: Four candidate lists of five items are offered as 'the five CI principles'. Which list is entirely correct?

options.text: Stability-based environments; maintain a repository; build every commit; self-testing build; keep it fast
Daily meeting; iteration meeting; release; baseline; backlog
Analogy; decomposition; tasking; baselines; release plan
