questions.explanation: Only the first list matches: stability-based environments, maintain a repository, build every commit, self-testing build, keep it fast. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which best explains the 'safeguarded' label applied to open-source development?

options.text: Commits pass through code-owner gating, like a centralized/safeguarded process

questions.explanation: Open source is safeguarded and uses code ownership with a wide community of variable skill. (TeamProcesses p.18)

questions.stem: With respect to risk, what does the deck name as a principal aim of planning?

options.text: Increasing the size variance of changes
Eliminating risk and uncertainty, helped by the repetitive nature of the process

questions.explanation: A main goal of planning is eliminating risk/uncertainty, aided by repetition. (IntroSwProcesses p.22)

questions.stem: Within the eight-step change process, Impact Analysis is one of which kind of granularity unit's phases?
questions.explanation: The software change is a task; IA is a phase within that task. (IntroSwProcesses p.4; ImpactAnalysis p.3)

questions.stem: The SIP enactment table walks one change through eight abbreviations. What does this demonstrate about the change process?

options.text: A software change is a repeatable task composed of ordered phases including IA

questions.explanation: The Pri/Ini/CL/IA/Ref/Ex/Act/Base walk shows the change as an ordered, repeatable task with IA as one phase. (IntroSwProcesses p.11)

questions.stem: In the Analysis Lab, after concept location students tabulate what about the codebase they explored?

options.text: Packages and the number of classes visited

questions.explanation: The portfolio table records package names and the number of classes visited (with comments). (AnalysisLab p.1-2)

questions.stem: Why does the guide treat the cash-register class names on the slides as stand-ins when applying IA to JHotDraw?

options.text: The store domain replaces JHotDraw in the exam
JHotDraw is not a Java codebase
The slides use the store domain pedagogically; the lab runs the same algorithm on the real JHotDraw graph
JHotDraw literally contains Store and Cashier classes

questions.explanation: Store/Cashier names are pedagogical; the Analysis Lab transfers the same marking mechanics to JHotDraw's interaction graph. (AnalysisLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the CI deck, which methods are named as the MOST effective for discovering defects?

options.text: Manual code reading by the author alone
Design inspections, code inspections, and testing

questions.explanation: The most effective defect-discovery methods are design inspections, code inspections, and testing. (ContinuousIntegration p.8)

questions.stem: Why are unit tests called the best method for verifying CI builds?

options.text: They are fast and focused, run without a database or file system, and pinpoint problems
They are end-to-end and exercise the whole system slowly

questions.explanation: Unit tests are fast, focused, no I/O, and pinpoint problems — best for verifying builds. (ContinuousIntegration p.9)

questions.stem: What does code-coverage tooling measure in a CI build?

options.text: The number of commits per day
How much of the code the tests exercise (e.g. covered vs uncovered lines/branches)
How many bugs are guaranteed remaining
The compile time of the project

questions.explanation: Coverage tools (Emma, Cobertura, Clover) measure how much code the tests exercise. (ContinuousIntegration p.14)

questions.stem: In CI, why is the source-code repository called the 'source of record'?

options.text: It runs the unit tests itself
The build server only takes code from it, so it is the authoritative version, not any laptop
It stores the defect log only

questions.explanation: The repo is the source of record; the build server only pulls from it, defeating 'works on my box'. (ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: The CI definition centres on merging working copies to which target several times a day?
questions.explanation: CI merges all developers' working copies to a shared mainline several times a day. (CILab p.1; ContinuousIntegration p.2)

questions.stem: In CIP, the 'permission to commit' gate is enforced before code reaches which shared resource?

options.text: The repository/mainline that the build server reads from

questions.explanation: CIP gates commits (via architects/code owners) before code lands in the shared repo the build server uses. (TeamProcesses p.16; ContinuousIntegration p.6)

questions.stem: What does the principle 'maintain a code repository' provide for CI?

options.text: A single shared place to store and integrate all developers' code

questions.explanation: Maintaining a code repository gives CI one shared integration point for all code. (ContinuousIntegration p.3)

questions.stem: Which estimation technique breaks a change into phases and sums their estimates?
questions.explanation: Decomposition splits the change into phases and sums per-phase estimates. (IntroSwProcesses p.23)

questions.stem: What does the estimated impact set consist of when IA finishes?

options.text: All classes marked CHANGED or PROPAGATING

questions.explanation: When no NEXT remain, the estimated set is the CHANGED plus PROPAGATING classes. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)

questions.stem: Before any class can be seeded as CHANGED, the algorithm performs one preparatory action over the whole graph. What is it?

options.text: Create the interaction diagram and mark every class BLANK

questions.explanation: The preparatory step builds the interaction diagram and marks all classes BLANK, before seeding CHANGED. (ImpactAnalysis p.12)
